transcripts.transcript: Hello everyone. Welcome to a new episode of The Dissenter. I'm your host, as always, Ricardo Lops, and today I'm joined by Dr. Orna Donov. She's a sociologist, lecturer, writer, and feminist activist. She teaches at Tel Aviv University, Ben-Gurion University. And the academic College of Tel Aviv Yafu. Her field of study focuses on the social expectations projected on women, both those who are mothers and those who are not, especially by choice. And today we're going to focus mostly on her book Regretting Motherhood A Study. So Dr. Rodonov, welcome to the show. It's a big pleasure to everyone. Thank you very much for inviting me. So let me ask you this to start off with. So what motivated you to study this phenomenon of women who regret motherhood, and why do you think it is important for us to understand it? I conducted my first study about women here in Israel who don't want to be mothers, and there was one sentence that kept troubling me, and that's a certain promise to women that they will surely regret if they will not become mothers. And as a sociologist, I started to wonder whether we are facing here a political usage of emotions. That society uses regret in order to align women into motherhood and how come we never heard about the possibility that women might regret becoming mothers, so I decided to write my PhD about it. Uh, I thought that we can learn a lot about not only about regretting motherhood, but also about society, about things that we are taking for granted. And uh that causes a lot of pain and suffering in lives of women and also sometimes children due to it. Right, and I mean you talked about this typical idea that people try to inculcate in women, particularly also sometimes in men, but mostly in women, that they will regret not having children. audio_segments.transcript: Hello everyone. Welcome to a new episode of The Dissenter. I'm your host, as always, Ricardo Lops, and today I'm joined by Dr. Orna Donov. She's a sociologist, lecturer, writer, and feminist activist. She teaches at Tel Aviv University, Ben-Gurion University.

And the academic College of Tel Aviv Yafu. Her field of study focuses on the social expectations projected on women, both those who are mothers and those who are not, especially by choice. And today we're going to focus mostly on her book Regretting Motherhood A Study. So Dr. Rodonov, welcome to the show. It's a big pleasure to everyone.
Thank you very much for inviting me.
So let me ask you this to start off with. So what motivated you to study this phenomenon of women who regret motherhood, and why do you think it is important for us to understand it?

I conducted my first study about women here in Israel who don't want to be mothers, and there was one sentence that kept troubling me, and that's a certain promise to women that they will surely regret if they will not become mothers. And as a sociologist, I started to wonder whether we are facing here a political usage of emotions.

That society uses regret in order to align women into motherhood and how come we never heard about the possibility that women might regret becoming mothers, so I decided to write my PhD about it. Uh, I thought that we can learn a lot about not only about regretting motherhood, but also about society, about things that we are taking for granted.
And uh that causes a lot of pain and suffering in lives of women and also sometimes children due to it.

Right, and I mean you talked about this typical idea that people try to inculcate in women, particularly also sometimes in men, but mostly in women, that they will regret not having children. I mean, where do you think that this idea comes from and why do you think that it's so relevant in, I guess, every human society?
Yeah, um.
Look, society would like us to have children.

Uh, children is, uh, is considered to be strength, you know, high, higher birth rates are strength, uh, political, economical, uh, in military, uh, territory, uh, high birth rates are needed in any country, and society would like the right people to have, uh, and I'm quoting.

Unquote, of course that will have more children, so they are trying to control the ability of women to have children because it is needed and also I don't, I'm not familiar with a society that doesn't consider like a real, another quote unquote, a real femininity as going hand in hand with motherhood.
Uh, you know, this is in patriarchal societies and heteronormative societies, and they, um,

Uh, these children are needed and also it is needed in order to keep women in place, uh, in their places, so in our places, so we are being like monitored not to move to a different place other than being wives and mothers still.

Also today in 2026, even though it might be more subtle, it's not uh with the real power, but it's like a quiet power, and it's going through our minds and we are being frightened and we are being told that the right way to live life, it's like the, the, the heteronormative life.
And every, because we are considered uh female and we are considered like our biological destiny, like our destiny is coded in our biology.
And we are being told that there is no other destination or meaning in women's life other than becoming mothers, so regret.
Is uh being uh used.
In order to frighten us, not to go on an alternative path other than wives and mothers.

Yeah, and this idea that is certainly tied to more traditional gender roles of women being mothers, having children, and also taking care of them, of course. I mean it, it manifests in many different ways. I mean, it's not only the ways we tend to associate it with, like for example in religious settings where people

people might talk about even a mission, the mission of women, but also, I mean, even in secular societies, people talk about the role of women and also men, of course, but here we're focusing mostly on women.
The role of women, so I mean it can have different kinds of manifestations, but all of them are tied to gender roles that we or the gender role that women are assigned, right?

and we think we have made a mistake. It's kind of like rocking the boat and it's really frightening. So talking about it, it's people would rather not to hear it or like to deny that it exists. They are experiencing and when time will pass, they will be all right. There is no such a thing as regretting motherhood, so I think this is one thing, the sacredness of motherhood. But another thing is that I think that more generally speaking, many societies are afraid of this emotional stance of regret in general.

Um, it seems like, um, like redundant emotion, uh, if it's not about a sin or a crime, then people should not regret, we should not look back, we should like go ahead and, and, uh, and, and do what you need to do in order to keep on going. Don't look back.

Uh, don't have second thoughts, just move on. And these women are reminding us that of course regret exists and we can regret everything, and we do regret everything in life, and this is human life. They are 70 years old. They are mothers already for 30 or 40 years. They didn't change their mind. They still think it was a mistake.
